Strong onshore winds developed in advance of an approaching warm front. Winds were sustained at speeds of 25 to 35 mph with some gusts of 60 to 65 mph along the immediate coast.

Strong onshore winds developed in advance of an approaching cold front. Winds were sustained at speeds of 25 to 35 mph...with some gusts to 60 mph along the immediate coast. The winds snapped many tree limbs which fell on powerlines contributing to power outages. Nearly 10,000 customers lost power for various lengths of time during the event.

Strong onshore winds developed in advance of intensifying low pressure lifting north across Maine. Sustained winds reached 30 to 40 mph at times with gusts occasionally approaching 70 mph. Trees were blown down at various locations including the towns of Hancock...Blue Hill...Sorrento and Bass Harbor. Many tree limbs were also snapped. The falling trees and tree limbs landing on powerlines or bringing down the powerlines contributed to power outages.

Strong onshore winds developed in advance of intensifying low pressure lifting north across Maine. Sustained winds reached 30 to 40 mph at times with gusts occasionally exceeding 70 mph. A gust of 74 mph was reported at Lubec with a gust of 72 mph at West Quoddy. Trees were blown down at various locations...with many tree limbs also snapped. The falling trees and tree limbs landing on powerlines or bringing down the powerlines contributed to power outages.

The remnants of Hurricane Wilma tracking east of the region and a second low intensifying while tracking north along the coast brought significant precipitation to the region. These systems also drew enough cold air across the region...to change precipitation to snow across northern and higher terrain areas. Even several hundred feet of elevation had a significant impact on snow amounts. Snowfall totals of 3 to 8 inches were common across much of the region...with the greater totals generally across higher terrain and more northern areas. Localized snowfall totals across higher terrain areas ranged from 12 to 16 inches in spots. The heavy wet snow accumulating on conductors contributed to power outages. The weight of the snow brought down trees and tree limbs which fell across powerlines and also accumulated on the powerlines which further contributed to power outages. Around 25,000 customers lost power for varying amounts of time during the storm.

Intensifying low pressure lifting north across the region produced the second heavy rain event in a week. Rainfall totals across Piscataquis county generally ranged from 3 to 4 inches...with local totals to around 4.50 inches. This heavy rain falling on already saturated ground led to rapid run off into rivers and streams which rose quickly in response to the rains. The Piscataquis River at Dover-Foxcroft crested at around 13 feet...where flood stage is 11 feet. Many smaller rivers and streams also overflowed their banks...flooding many roads. The combination of run off from this rain and the heavy rain event just a week earlier...kept many rivers and steams at significantly elevated levels for several days following the rain event.

The remnants of Hurricane Wilma tracking east of the region and a second low intensifying while tracking north along the coast brought strong winds to the region. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported near the immediate coast...with 30 to 40 mph gusts across interior Downeast areas. The combination of strong winds and saturated ground from several weeks of heavy rain allowed trees to be blown down along with many branches. The falling trees and branches also brought down powerlines which resulted in many power outages. Several thousand customers lost power for varying amounts of time during the storm.

Intensifying low pressure lifting north across the region produced the second heavy rain event in a week. Rainfall totals across Aroostook county generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches...with local totals to around 4.50 inches. The heaviest rains fell across east and northeast portions of the county. This heavy rain falling on already saturated ground led to rapid run off into rivers and streams which rose quickly in response to the rains. A beaver dam on the Dead Brook near Bridgewater collapsed...releasing impounded water from the recent heavy rains. These waters flooded a portion of Route 1 in Bridgewater and spilled into the firehouse near the brook with 2 feet of water reported in the building. Elevated river and stream levels were reported...particularly across eastern portions of the county...with some smaller rivers and streams overflowing their banks. Some roads were closed due to flooding...with many reports of standing water in poor drainage areas. Water was also reported in many basements. The combination of run off from this rain and the heavy rain event just a week earlier...kept many rivers and streams at elevated levels for several days following the rain event.

Heavy rain fell over Maine due to the rapid development of surface low pressure well to the southeast of New England. Rainfall amounts across the area ranged from around 3 inches to over 5 inches. The heavy rain caused minor flooding on some of the large rivers.The Presumpscot River at Westbrook (Flood Stage 15.0 ft) crested at 18.65 ft. The Swift River at Roxbury (Flood Stage 7.0 ft) crested at 9.02 ft.  The Kennebec River crested at 46523 cfs at Skowhegan (Flood Stage 35000 cfs), 19.62 ft at North Sidney (Flood Stage 17.0 ft),  and at 15.85 ft at Augusta (Flood Stage 13.0 ft).

The Kennebec River crested at 46523 cfs at Skowhegan (Flood Stage 35000 cfs), 19.62 ft at North Sidney (Flood Stage 17.0 ft),  and at 15.85 ft at Augusta (Flood Stage 13.0 ft).

Intensifying low pressure lifting north across the region produced the second heavy rain event in a week. Rainfall totals across Aroostook county generally ranged from 2 to 4 inches...with local totals to around 4.50 inches. The heaviest rains fell across east and northeast portions of the county. This heavy rain falling on already saturated ground led to rapid run off into rivers and streams which rose quickly in response to the rains. A beaver dam on the Dead Brook near Bridgewater collapsed...releasing impounded water from the recent heavy rains. These waters flooded a portion of Route 1 in Bridgewater and spilled into the firehouse near the brook with 2 feet of water reported in the building. Elevated river and stream levels were reported...particularly across eastern portions of the county...with some smaller rivers and streams overflowing their banks. Some roads were closed due to flooding...with many reports of standing water in poor drainage areas. Water was also reported in many basements. The combination of run off from this rain and the heavy rain event just a week earlier...kept many rivers and streams at elevated levels for several days following the rain event.

The remnants of Hurricane Wilma tracking east of the region and a second low intensifying while tracking north along the coast brought strong winds to the region. Wind gusts of 50 to 60 mph were reported near the immediate coast...with 30 to 40 mph gusts across interior Downeast areas. The combination of strong winds and saturated ground from several weeks of heavy rain allowed trees to be blown down along with many branches. The falling trees and branches also brought down powerlines which resulted in many power outages. Several thousand customers lost power for varying amounts of time during the storm.

The interaction between a cold frontal boundary and the remnants of Tropical Storm Tammy resulted in tremendous rainfall throughout most southern and coastal Maine. Rainfall amounts ranged from around 2 inches in the mountains to 11.92 inches at Brunswick. The rainfall caused flooding of small rivers and streams resulting in road washouts.

An area of low pressure that developed over the Gulf States on the morning of the 15th moved northeast to near New York City by the morning of the 16th spreading snow into the state.  Snow, mixed with sleet in places, continued throughout much of the day in the mountains and foothills as the low continued to move northeastward through the Gulf of Maine.  Along the coast and inland of the mid-coast, warmer air streaming northward aloft on southerly winds caused the snow to change to a mixture of rain, sleet, and freezing rain.  Snowfall amounts ranged from 4 to 8 inches across the mountains, foothills and western interior zones to almost nothing along the immediate coast and inland from the mid-coast where the majority of the precipitation fell as rain.

An area of low pressure developed off the mid-Atlantic coast in the early morning hours of the 9th and moved rapidly northeastward during the day.  The system brought heavy snow to portions of southern and coastal sections of the state.  Snowfall began in southwestern Maine shortly after daybreak and continued into the evening, especially in the mid-coast area.  Snowfall amounts along the coast ranged from 6 to 12 inches with locally higher amounts (up to 17 inches) in coastal areas of York County and Cumberland Counties.  Farther inland, snowfall amounts tapered off rapidly with only 2 to 4 inches in the mountains.

A slow moving cold front moved through northern New England on November 30th. Precipitation amounts of 1 to 2 inches caused minor flooding on the Kennebec and Presumpscot Rivers on December 1st.  No damage occurred in this flooding. The Kennebec River crested at Skowhegan (flood stage 35000 cfs) at 43648 cfs, at North Sidney (flood stage 17 ft) at 17.5 ft, and at Augusta (flood stage 13.0 ft) at 14.0 ft. The Presumpscot River at Westbrook (flood stage 15.0 ft) crested at 15.7 ft.

A series of lows lifted across the region from November 30th through December 2nd. Liquid precipitation totals during this time generally ranged from 2.00 to 4.00 inches across Aroostook county. The heaviest rains generally occurred across eastern portions of the county during December 1st and 2nd with 1.50 to 2.50 inches of rain reported...with lesser rainfall totals across northwest portions of the county. With the soil already very wet from the recent rains and a very rainy Fall season...runoff from these rains and melting snow quickly reached area rivers and streams. Smaller rivers and streams rose quickly during the 2nd in response to the most recent rains and melting snow...with some overflowing their banks. Portions of some roads were closed due to flooding...with flooding also reported in poor drainage areas. Most of the flooding was considered minor.

An early season storm brought a mixture of snow, sleet, and rain to the area.  Heavy snow fell in the western mountains, especially in the higher elevations where over a foot of new snow was reported.  Rangeley reported 16 inches of snowfall.  Just rain fell across the coast and interior zones.

High surf from a coastal storm caused flooding and/or erosion of roads in the towns of Saco, Kennebunk, Wells and York.  Several of the coastal roads were closed due to the "splashover" flooding and because rocks had washed onto the roadway.

High winds knocked down numerous trees and limbs which, in turn, knocked down power lines throughout the area.  Central Maine Power Company reported between 20,000-30,000 customers without electrical service.A peak wind gust of 60 mph was observed at the National Weather Service office in Gray (Cumberland County) with 47 mph reported in Poland (Androscoggin County) and 43 mph in Brunswick (Cumberland County).Trees and/or large branches were reported down in Rangeley, Madrid, and New Portland and in sections of Northern Oxford and Southern Somerset Counties.

Intensifying low pressure lifting north across Maine brought heavy rain to the region. With soil conditions already wet following a very rainy October and early November...runoff from these rains rapidly caused rising water levels on area rivers and streams. Rainfall totals across Piscataquis county generally ranged from 1.50 to 3.00 inches...with the greater totals occurring across favored upslope areas. Many smaller rivers and streams did overflow their banks to varying extents and in some instances flooded portions of roads...with a few roads also experiencing small wash outs in spots. Ponding of water was reported in many poor drainage areas. Water was also reported in many basements. Though larger rivers rose...they remained within their banks. Most of the flooding was considered minor...though flooding was reported in many locations.

A pair of waterspouts/tornadoes moved northward from the Gulf of Maine during the early afternoon of Thanksgiving Day.  Earlier on that day, light snow had been falling across the area.  The waterspouts/tornadoes formed along a warm frontal boundary that was associated with an area of low pressure which was bringing snow to much of the state.  The first waterspout/tornado moved ashore over the southern tip of the Phippsburg peninsula near Bald Head, crossed Small Point, moved over The Branch, went ashore again on Hermit Island, lifted, reformed over Tottman Cove, then went ashore once again near West Point, and then caused sporadic damage along Holland Road.  Numerous large trees were snapped or uprooted throughout the area by the tornado, especially in the area near Small Point.  In addition, several homes near Small Point were blown off their foundations and significantly damaged.  The second tornado/waterspout reportedly touched down as a waterspout south of Brunswick in Middle Bay, then moved ashore near the northern end of Mere Point Neck, moved back over water at the northern end of Maquoit Bay, then moved back over land and crossed Bunganug Road before dissipating.  Damage to the area was mostly snapped or uprooted trees, although one camper cover was torn and lifted by the tornado, and landed about a mile away in the top of a tall tree.

Intensifying low pressure tracking through the Gulf of Maine brought snow to the region. Snow began during the afternoon of the 16th...with storm total snow accumulations of 6 to 10 inches reported through the early morning of the 17th.

Intensifying low pressure tracking through the Gulf of Maine brought snow to the region. Snow began during the afternoon of the 16th...with storm total snow accumulations of 6 to 10 inches reported through the early morning of the 17th.

Slow moving low pressure intensified while tracking along the Maine coast to Nova Scotia from the 26th to the 27th. Snow developed in advance of this low during the morning of December 25th. Snow continued through the 25th...with increasing intensities starting later on the 25th. Snow...heavy at times...continued through the 26th with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times. Winds increased to 20 to 30 mph during the 26th...with similar wind speeds continuing into the 27th. These winds contributed to extensive blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall intensities began to diminish late on the 26th...with lighter snows continuing through much of the morning of the 27th. Storm total snow accumulations during the 48 hours of this event ranged from 30 to 40 inches across northern portions of Aroostook county. Some specific snow totals included 40 inches at Clayton Lake...38 inches at Plaisted...36 to 37 inches at Madawaska and Fort Kent...31 to  32 inches at Caribou and Portage and 30 inches at Washburn. Precipitation in these areas was snow for much of this event...though the snow did mix with sleet and freezing rain at times particularly from Caribou to Portage to Washburn. Storm total snow accumulations decreased to the south of Caribou where a greater portion of the precipitation fell in the form of sleet and freezing rain. In the Presque Isle area...10 miles south of Caribou...storm total snow accumulations of 24 to 30 inches were reported. Another 10 miles to the south of Presque Isle...around 18 inches of snow were reported at Mars Hill.The combination of heavy snow...along with extensive blowing and drifting snow...made roads nearly impassable at times across portions of the Saint John Valley in northernmost Aroostook county. The combination of heavy snow accumulating on trees and gusty winds snapped some branches which fell on powerlines contributing to power outages. The weight of heavy snow and ice building up on powerlines also brought down some lines.  The event storm total snow accumulations from the Caribou area northward to the Saint John Valley were record or near record values.

Slow moving low pressure intensified while tracking along the Maine coast to Nova Scotia from the 26th to the 27th. Snow developed in advance of this low during the morning of December 25th. Snow continued through the 25th...with increasing intensities starting later on the 25th. Snow...heavy at times...continued through the 26th with snowfall rates of 2 to 3 inches per hour at times. Winds increased to 20 to 30 mph during the 26th...with similar wind speeds continuing into the 27th. These winds contributed to extensive blowing and drifting snow. Snowfall intensities began to diminish late on the 26th...with lighter snows continuing through much of the morning of the 27th. Storm total snow accumulations during the 48 hours of this event ranged from 30 to 40 inches across northern portions of Aroostook county. Some specific snow totals included 40 inches at Clayton Lake...38 inches at Plaisted...36 to 37 inches at Madawaska and Fort Kent...31 to  32 inches at Caribou and Portage and 30 inches at Washburn. Precipitation in these areas was snow for much of this event...though the snow did mix with sleet and freezing rain at times particularly from Caribou to Portage to Washburn. Storm total snow accumulations decreased to the south of Caribou where a greater portion of the precipitation fell in the form of sleet and freezing rain. In the Presque Isle area...10 miles south of Caribou...storm total snow accumulations of 24 to 30 inches were reported. Another 10 miles to the south of Presque Isle...around 18 inches of snow were reported at Mars Hill.The combination of heavy snow...along with extensive blowing and drifting snow...made roads nearly impassable at times across portions of the Saint John Valley in northernmost Aroostook county. The combination of heavy snow accumulating on trees and gusty winds snapped some branches which fell on powerlines contributing to power outages. The weight of heavy snow and ice building up on powerlines also brought down some lines.  The event storm total snow accumulations from the Caribou area northward to the Saint John Valley were record or near record values.

A pair of waterspouts/tornadoes moved northward from the Gulf of Maine during the early afternoon of Thanksgiving Day.  Earlier on that day, light snow had been falling across the area.  The waterspouts/tornadoes formed along a warm frontal boundary that was associated with an area of low pressure which was bringing snow to much of the state.  The first waterspout/tornado moved ashore over the southern tip of the Phippsburg peninsula near Bald Head, crossed Small Point, moved over The Branch, went ashore again on Hermit Island, lifted, reformed over Tottman Cove, then went ashore once again near West Point, and then caused sporadic damage along Holland Road.  Numerous large trees were snapped or uprooted throughout the area by the tornado, especially in the area near Small Point.  In addition, several homes near Small Point were blown off their foundations and significantly damaged.  The second tornado/waterspout reportedly touched down as a waterspout south of Brunswick in Middle Bay, then moved ashore near the northern end of Mere Point Neck, moved back over water at the northern end of Maquoit Bay, then moved back over land and crossed Bunganug Road before dissipating.  Damage to the area was mostly snapped or uprooted trees, although one camper cover was torn and lifted by the tornado, and landed about a mile away in the top of a tall tree.

Low pressure developed along the southeast coast of the United States on the morning of November 21 and moved northeast spreading heavy rain into Maine. Precipitation ranged from 2 to 3 inches. Minor flooding resulted with no reported damage.The Presumpscot river at Westbrook (Flood Stage 15.0 ft) crested at 16.2 ft. and the Kennebec river at Skowhegan (Flood Stage 35000 cfs) briefly rose to 35000 cfs.

Intensifying low pressure lifting north across Maine brought heavy rain to the region. With soil conditions already wet following a very rainy October and early November...runoff from these rains rapidly caused rising water levels on area rivers and streams. Rainfall totals across Hancock county generally ranged from 1.50 to 3.0 inches.  Rainfall totals across Penobscot county generally ranged from 2.0 to 3.0 inches...with local totals in excess of 4.0 inches across favored upslope areas in northern portions of the county. Rainfall totals across Washington county generally ranged from 2.0 to 3.0 inches...with local totals in excess of 4.0 inches. Many smaller rivers and streams did overflow their banks to varying extents and in some cases flooded portions of roads...with a few roads also experiencing small wash outs in spots. Ponding of water was reported in many poor drainage areas. Water was also reported in many basements. Though the larger rivers rose...they remained within their banks. Strong onshore winds funelling up Penobscot Bay...piled water into the lower reaches of the Penobscot River which at high tide contributed to some minor flooding along the lower reaches of the Penobscot river and surrounding tributaries.  Most of the flooding across the region was considered minor...though flooding was reported in many locations.

Strong winds developed in advance of intensifying low pressure lifting north across Maine. Sustained winds reached 25 to 35 mph at times with occasional gusts of 60 to 65 mph. Trees were blown down at various locations including the town of Calais where one tree fell on a truck. Many tree limbs were also snapped. The falling trees and tree limbs landing on powerlines or bringing down the powerlines contributed to power outages.

Slow moving low pressure intensified while tracking along the Maine coast to Nova Scotia from the 26th to the 27. Snow developed in advance of this low during the morning of the 25th...then transitioned to sleet and freezing rain with warmer air being drawn across the region later on the 25th. A mix of snow...sleet and freezing rain which was heavy at times then continued into the 26th. Precipitation transitioned back to snow later on the 26th when colder air was drawn back across the region...with snow then continuing into the 27th. Storm total snow accumulations during the 48 hours of this event generally ranged from 10 to 20 inches across the region...with the lesser totals across southern and eastern areas. Ice accumulations of around 0.5 inch were also reported...along with significant sleet accumulations. Winds increased to 20 to 30 mph during the 26th...with similar wind speeds continuing into the 27th which contributed to areas of blowing and drifting snow.

Winds increased to 20 to 30 mph during the 26th...with similar winds continuing into the 27th which contributed to areas of blowing and drifting snow.

Sleet accumulations of between 0.5 and 1.0 inch...along with significant ice accumulations from freezing rain...were also reported. Winds increased to 20 to 30 mph during the 26th...with similar winds continuing into the 27th which contributed to areas of blowing and drifting snow.

A series of lows lifted across the region from November 30th through December 2nd. Liquid precipitation totals during this time generally ranged from 3.00 to 5.00 inches across Washington county and from 1.50 to 3.50 inches across Penobscot county. The heaviest rains generally occurred during December 1st and 2nd when 2.00 to 3.00 inches of rain were reported across Washington county with 0.50 to 1.50 inches across Penobscot county.  With the soil already very wet from recent rains and a very rainy Fall season...runoff from these rains and any melting snow quickly reached area rivers and streams. Smaller rivers and streams rose quickly during the 2nd in response to the most recent rains and melting snow...with some overflowing their banks. Portions of some roads were closed due to flooding...with flooding also reported in poor drainage areas. Most of the flooding was considered minor.

Low pressure tracking along the Maine coast to Nova Scotia brought a wintry mix to extreme northern and northwest Maine. Precipitation began in the form of snow during the early morning hours of the 29th...then transitioned to a mix of snow...sleet and freezing rain. Precipitation then transitioned back to snow with additional accumulations on the 30th before ending. Storm total snow accumulations across the region ranged from 5 to 9 inches. Ice accumulations of up to between 0.5 and 0.75 of an inch from freezing rain and sleet accumulations of between 1.0 and 1.5 inches were also reported.
